source.title: Combined machine learning models, docking analysis, ADMET studies and molecular dynamics simulations for the design of novel FAK inhibitors against glioblastoma

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study aims to design novel FAK inhibitors for glioblastoma (GBM) by integrating machine learning, docking, ADMET, and MD simulations. GBM is highly aggressive with poor prognosis, and FAK is a promising target. The research gap is the lack of large-dataset QSAR models for FAK inhibitors. The approach uses two ML models (protein-level and cell-level) to screen a large compound library, followed by docking, ADMET filtering, and MD validation to identify drug-like candidates.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the entire study: integrated ML, docking, ADMET, and MD to identify novel FAK inhibitors for GBM. ML models achieved R² 0.892 (protein-level) and 0.789 (cell-level). Screened 5107 compounds, identified 275 active, 16 passed ADMET, and MD confirmed stable binding.
process_signals: Integrated workflow: ML → docking → ADMET → MD
Two ML models: protein-level (1280 compounds) and cell-level (2608 compounds)
Screening funnel: 5107 → 275 → 16 compounds
metrics: R², MAE, RMSE for ML models
ADMET drug-likeness criteria (Lipinski, Pfizer, GSK, Golden Triangle)
RMSD, binding free energy for MD
failures_or_limits: Only 16 out of 275 compounds passed all ADMET filters
No experimental validation

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: GBM is aggressive with poor prognosis; FAK is a key target. Current QSAR models for FAK inhibitors use small datasets (tens to hundreds), limiting applicability. This study proposes a comprehensive framework using larger datasets (>1000) and integrated CADD methods.
process_signals: Problem: GBM resistance, need for novel therapies
Target: FAK, involved in tumor progression
Gap: existing QSAR models have small datasets
Hypothesis: larger dataset ML models can enable rapid screening
failures_or_limits: Previous QSAR models had limited dataset sizes (e.g., 22 compounds)

section_gists.heading: Development of the database
section_gists.gist: Data collected from ChEMBL: 1280 FAK inhibitors (protein-level) and 2608 compounds against U87-MG cells. IC50 values averaged if within 10 µM range; pIC50 used as target. Structural diversity analyzed via plain rings and Murcko scaffolds.
process_signals: Data source: ChEMBL (target CHEMBL2695, cell line CHEMBL3307575)
Filtering: only specific IC50 values; averaged if variation ≤10 µM; excluded if >10 µM
Target variable: pIC50 (-log10(IC50))
Structural analysis: DataWarrior for rings and scaffolds
failures_or_limits: Exclusion of compounds with large IC50 variations may bias dataset
No mention of handling duplicates or assay types

section_gists.heading: Molecular descriptions calculation and machine learning models building
section_gists.gist: Molecular fingerprints (CDK, CDK extended, substructure, substructure counts) computed with PaDEL. Models: LightGBM, RF, SVR, KNN, PLS, LASSO, XGBoost. 80/20 split, 10-fold CV, grid search for hyperparameters. Metrics: R², MAE, RMSE.
process_signals: Fingerprint choice: four types from PaDEL
Algorithm priority: LightGBM for ensemble advantages
Validation: 10-fold CV to mitigate random split effects
Hyperparameter tuning: grid search
methods: LightGBM, RF, SVR, KNN, PLS, LASSO, XGBoost
failures_or_limits: No mention of data leakage or applicability domain

section_gists.heading: Molecular docking studies
section_gists.gist: FAK crystal structure (PDB: 2JKK) prepared by removing water and ligand. Docking with AutoDock Vina; grid box 22.5 Å³ centered on co-crystallized ligand. Binding energy used as activity measure. Interactions analyzed with Discovery Studio.
process_signals: Docking validation: redocking of TAE-226 (RMSD 0.581 Å, score -9.613 kcal/mol)
Screening: ChemDiv library (1.6 million compounds) docked; cutoff -9.0 kcal/mol selected 5107 compounds
failures_or_limits: Docking score cutoff may miss weaker binders with good kinetics

section_gists.heading: ADME prediction
section_gists.gist: ADMET properties predicted using ADMETlab 2.0. Drug-likeness evaluated by Lipinski, Pfizer, GSK, Golden Triangle rules.
process_signals: Early ADMET assessment to avoid late-stage failures
Four drug-likeness criteria applied
metrics: Lipinski, Pfizer, GSK, Golden Triangle rules
failures_or_limits: In silico ADMET predictions may not fully replicate in vivo behavior

section_gists.heading: MD simulation
section_gists.gist: MD simulations with Gromacs 2022. Force fields: GAFF for ligands, AMBER14SB for protein, TIP3P water. 100 ns production after NVT/NPT equilibration. Analysis: RMSD, MM-PBSA binding free energy.
process_signals: Selected compounds 1 and 2 for MD
Stable RMSD indicates binding stability
MM-PBSA: van der Waals dominates binding
failures_or_limits: Only two compounds simulated; limited sampling
No mention of convergence criteria

section_gists.heading: Development of ML models for FAK inhibitors
section_gists.gist: Best model: LightGBM with combined CDK, CDK extended, substructure count fingerprints. Test set R²=0.892, MAE=0.331, RMSE=0.467. Feature importance: SubFPC307 (chiral center) top. LightGBM outperformed other algorithms.
process_signals: Combined fingerprints improved performance over single ones
Optimal input: CDK+CDK extended+substructure count (2355 bits)
LightGBM best among tested algorithms
Feature importance highlights chiral centers, tautomerizable groups, rotatable bonds
failures_or_limits: Model may overfit (training R²=0.990)
No external validation set

section_gists.heading: Development of ML models for U-87 MG glioblastomas
section_gists.gist: Cell-level model also best with LightGBM and same fingerprint combination. Test set R²=0.789, MAE=0.395, RMSE=0.536. Feature importance similar but includes heterocyclic features. LightGBM again best.
process_signals: Consistent optimal fingerprint combination across both models
Cell-level model slightly lower performance (R²=0.789 vs 0.892)
Feature importance: chiral center, tautomerizable, conserved bond, rotatable bond, primary carbon, etc.
failures_or_limits: Lower R² may indicate more noise in cell-based data
No discussion of discrepancy between protein and cell models

section_gists.heading: Molecular docking analysis
section_gists.gist: Docking validation: TAE-226 redocked with RMSD 0.581 Å. ChemDiv library docked; 5107 compounds with score ≤ -9.0 kcal/mol. ML models predicted pIC50; 275 compounds had pIC50 >6.0 for both protein and cell models. Chemical space diverse.
process_signals: Validation ensures docking reliability
Cutoff -9.0 kcal/mol based on reference
Intersection of docking and ML predictions: 275 compounds
Chemical space analysis shows diversity
failures_or_limits: Only 5.38% of docked compounds passed ML filters
No discussion of false positives/negatives

section_gists.heading: ADME prediction analysis
section_gists.gist: 275 compounds screened with ADMETlab 2.0; 16 passed all four drug-likeness rules. Most have good HIA, Caco-2, MDCK permeability; variable PPB and BBB. Some CYP substrate liabilities. Compound 15 may be mutagenic. Binding patterns show key residues (Cys502, Asp564). Low similarity to known FAK inhibitors.
process_signals: ADMET filters drastically reduced candidates (275→16)
Drug-likeness rules: Lipinski, Pfizer, GSK, Golden Triangle
Key interactions: H-bonds with Cys502, Asp564; π-σ with Leu553
Novelty: low Tanimoto similarity to known FAK ligands
metrics: HIA, Caco-2, MDCK, PPB, VD, BBB, CYP, Ames, T1/2
failures_or_limits: Only 16 compounds passed all filters; many failed due to ADMET
Compound 15 mutagenic potential
No experimental ADMET validation

section_gists.heading: MD simulation analysis
section_gists.gist: MD of compounds 1 and 2 with FAK showed stable RMSD. MM-PBSA: binding free energies -58.32 and -96.41 kJ/mol. Van der Waals dominates. Confirms stable binding.
process_signals: RMSD stable after ~20 ns
Binding free energy favorable
Van der Waals main contributor
failures_or_limits: Only two compounds simulated
No entropy convergence check
No experimental binding affinity

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study follows a linear funnel: large dataset ML models → docking screening → ADMET filtering → MD validation.
The choice of LightGBM and combined fingerprints was driven by empirical performance comparison.
The low yield (16/5107) suggests stringent filters, but may also indicate model limitations.
The authors emphasize novelty of hits via low similarity to known inhibitors.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why was the docking score cutoff set to -9.0 kcal/mol? Is it based on TAE-226?
How were the 1.6 million ChemDiv compounds selected for docking?
Were there any compounds that passed ML but failed docking, or vice versa?
What is the applicability domain of the ML models?
Why were only two compounds chosen for MD?
Are there plans for experimental validation?

events.summary: Evaluated impact of different molecular fingerprint combinations on LightGBM model performance for FAK inhibitors using 10-fold cross-validation.

Analyzed feature importance for U-87 MG model; SubFPC307 (chiral center) again top, with additional heterocyclic features.

ML models predict pIC50 for 5107 compounds; 275 meet dual criteria (pIC50 >6.0 for both protein and cell).

events.summary: MD simulations show stable RMSD for compound 1 and compound 2 binding to FAK.
numbers_or_metrics: RMSD values from 0.22 to 0.27 nm over 100 ns

events.summary: MD simulations confirm stable binding of selected compounds to FAK protein.
Further experimental validation through in vitro and in vivo studies is required.
The study integrated machine learning, docking, and ADMET to identify 16 potential FAK inhibitors from 5107 candidates.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains MD simulation results, binding free energy analysis, and conclusion summarizing the workflow and need for experimental validation.

evidence_bank.quote: Gliomas, particularly glioblastoma (GBM), are highly aggressive brain tumors with poor prognosis and high recurrence rates.
the prognosis for GBM patients has seen only marginal improvement over the past few decades. The median survival time remains disappointingly low, typically not exceeding 15 months
One promising target is Focal adhesion kinase (FAK), a key regulator of tumor progression currently in clinical trials for glioma treatment.

the current QSAR models used to predict FAK inhibition activity have relatively small construction datasets, typically comprising only tens to hundreds of compounds, which somewhat limits the widespread applicability of the models.
Wang et al. utilized 22 FAK inhibitors to develop two types of satisfactory 3D-QSAR models

it is particularly important and urgent to construct a predictive model based on a relatively large dataset (with more than 1000 data points) to enable rapid screening of FAK inhibitors for glioblastoma.

we propose a comprehensive research framework that integrates machine learning models, docking analysis, ADMET studies, and molecular dynamics simulations to facilitate the design of novel FAK inhibitors against glioblastoma
The regression models leveraged LightGBM, Random Forest, SVR, KNN, PLS, LASSO, and XGBoost algorithms.
The datasets were split into training and independent test sets, maintaining an 80:20 ratio. Ten-fold Cross-validation was implemented during model training

The outcomes indicated that using a single molecular fingerprint resulted in lower predictive performance compared to combined molecular fingerprints, emphasizing the complementarity of combined fingerprints in enhancing the representation of relevant molecular structural information.
The model achieved the highest predictive accuracy when integrating combined CDK, CDK extended, and substructure fingerprint counts.
Although combining all molecular fingerprints could yield similar results, the computational efficiency of utilizing these three fingerprints guided the selection for subsequent model development.
SubFPC307 indicates the presence of a specified chiral center, highlighting a specific atom in the molecule exhibiting chirality.
It is noteworthy that the LightGBM algorithm produces the lowest MAE and RMSE values, highlighting its superior predictive accuracy.
Table 2 illustrates the evaluation results of the machine learning models using cross-validation on the test set with the LightGBM approach.
The results demonstrate that the highest model performance is attained when CDK, CDK extended fingerprints, and substructure fingerprint counts are employed as input variables.

Using these models, we efficiently identified 275 potentially active compounds out of 5107 candidates based on docking scores lower than -9.000 kcal/mol. Following ADMET assessments, 16 compounds stood out as potential FAK inhibitors.
These results indicate that molecular fingerprint models based on LightGBM are proficient in predicting the activity ( $pIC_{50}$ ) of compounds against U-87 MG glioblastomas.
The results demonstrate that TAE-226, used as a positive control, forms strong interactions with FAK through hydrogen bonds involving the Cys502 and Asp564 residues.

Specifically, TAE-226 forms $\pi$ - $\sigma$ interactions with the Leu553 residue, and statistical data (Table S11) suggest that other compounds predominantly interact with Leu553 through $\pi$ - $\sigma$ interactions, highlighting its significant role.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Design novel FAK inhibitors for glioblastoma by integrating machine learning, docking, ADMET, and MD simulations to overcome limitations of small-dataset QSAR models.

canonical_events.summary: GBM is highly aggressive with poor prognosis, necessitating novel therapies.
FAK is a promising target for GBM therapy.
Existing QSAR models for FAK inhibitors use small datasets, limiting applicability.
A larger dataset ML model can enable rapid screening of FAK inhibitors for GBM.
Proposed integrated framework: ML models, docking, ADMET, and MD simulations.
Built ML models using multiple algorithms and four fingerprint types from PaDEL; 80/20 split, 10-fold CV.
Evaluated fingerprint combinations for FAK inhibitor model; combined CDK, CDK extended, substructure count fingerprints gave best performance.
FAK inhibitor model achieved test set R²=0.892, MAE=0.331, RMSE=0.467; LightGBM outperformed other algorithms.
Feature importance for FAK model: SubFPC307 (chiral center) ranked top.
Built cell-level model for U-87 MG glioblastoma; same optimal fingerprint combination; test set R²=0.789, MAE=0.395, RMSE=0.536.
Docking validation: redocking TAE-226 gave RMSD 0.581 Å, confirming protocol reliability.
Virtual screening of 1.6 million ChemDiv compounds by docking; 5107 selected with score ≤ -9.0 kcal/mol.
ML models predicted pIC50 for 5107 compounds; 275 met dual criteria (pIC50 >6.0 for both protein and cell models).
ADMET prediction filtered 275 candidates to 16 passing all four drug-likeness rules (Lipinski, Pfizer, GSK, Golden Triangle).
Binding pattern analysis revealed key interactions with Cys502, Asp564, and Leu553; low similarity to known FAK inhibitors.
MD simulations of compounds 1 and 2 showed stable RMSD and favorable binding free energies (ΔG_bind -58.32 and -96.41 kJ/mol).
Further experimental validation through in vitro and in vivo studies is required.

edges.reason: Poor prognosis of GBM motivates targeting FAK.
After identifying FAK as target, existing QSAR limitations are noted.
Small dataset limitation leads to hypothesis that larger dataset ML can improve screening.
Hypothesis motivates design of integrated framework.
Framework design specifies ML model building with multiple algorithms and fingerprints.
Model building leads to evaluation of fingerprint combinations.
Optimal fingerprint selection yields final FAK model performance.
After performance assessment, feature importance is analyzed.
Same modeling approach applied to cell-level data.
After building models, docking validation is performed.
Validated docking protocol used for virtual screening.
Docked compounds are filtered by ML predictions.
ADMET-passed compounds are analyzed for binding patterns.
MD validation confirms stability, but experimental validation is still needed.

must_cover_points: GBM poor prognosis and need for novel therapies
Limitations of existing small-dataset QSAR models
Hypothesis that larger dataset ML models can enable rapid screening
ML model building with multiple algorithms and fingerprints
Selection of optimal fingerprint combination (CDK+CDK extended+substructure count)
Virtual screening of 1.6M compounds to 5107 hits
MD validation of two compounds showing stable binding
